speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. Today is September 25th, 1996. This is an interview with Bernhard Storch. As we ended the last tape, we were talking about your being alone in the apartment. Your uncle and his family were on vacation, and you woke up in-- at around 4:00 in the morning and saw German soldiers. Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened-- I got dressed. The town-- the-- what-- the city-- what do you call that? The city government was just walking distance from my-- from the apartment. By the way, the apartment where I lived-- the street which I live was by the name of [POLISH]. [POLISH]. I will spell it. G-O-- G-- no. G-L-O-W-W-A-C-K-O. No. [POLISH]. See. I forgot how to spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me repeat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --we wrote it down before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We wrote down. OK. Anyway, [POLISH] three, and that was Chorzow. And it was right near the square where the building-- the government buildings were there. Now, I actually-- I suspected something before that, because they were moving out documents. But we didn't know if that's for the war, or if they simply have not enough room there. So they were moving out boxes. This we noticed a week before. But as far as the-- what I did right after, the-- I saw those six Germans. I got dressed, and I went to the street-- on the street, and watch what was going on. And, of course, at that particular time there was-- already the morning newspaper came out. And it stated that Poland was attacked during the night. It was just a blur. In other words, just a headline. No more detail. Just the headline. My first instinct was, of course, to go home, because I was by myself. My uncle and his wife and their little baby were on vacation, and they were visiting his parents, which was also in my hometown, Bochnia. And they were there. And my first instinct was to call them. Unfortunately, I could not get through with the telephone. The line was disrupted to Bochnia. So the second instinct was to call the-- the railroad station about the timetable, when I can go to Bochnia. In other words, you couldn't go directly to Bochnia. You had to go first to cracow, and then take another plane, a local plane to Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called the railroad station. And they said the last-- the next train will be going approximately at 1:30-- approximately at 1:30 in the afternoon. So I packed my little suitcase whatever I could, and I was going home. I didn't need too much, because I had some things there. So I was going home, and that's exactly what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home. I went to the station to Katowice, took the trolley car, which was only 10 kilometers away. And from Katowice, I bought a ticket to Cracow, and we went on the train. Now, normally a trip like that would take me close to three hours, less than three hours from the Chorzow to Cracow. Unfortunately, when we started out, the German planes attacked the trains-- everything which moved. They attacked the trains. And there were a lot of disruptions. There were a lot of people-- as soon as they were taking the train, the engineers stopped the train. And people jumped out from the train onto the cars and, you know, seeking shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the trip itself, a lot of people got killed, injured. But the trip itself, instead of taking-- take me two hours, took me almost two days. So we arrived finally to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunate, in Cracow, there was no passenger trains going down west anymore. So my instinct told me to jump on any kind of train which faced-- with the, the locomotive faces the direction south. And I did. I went in a-- on a-- not on a passenger train, just I jump on a train, and that's it. And hoping that it was going the right direction. It could have turned left and go to Warsaw. But fortunately for me, I suppose somebody was watching me. [LAUGHS] I jump on the train without my suitcase. I lost the suitcase. I lost the suitcase. And I was on the train. And, you know, from-- from Cracow to Bochnia would have taken about 35 minutes normal. But that was not [LAUGHS] normal time in those days. So we arrive. Finally. I did arrive to Bochnia on May 3rd, which was three days--

speaker: interviewer
text: September 3rd.

speaker: interviewee
text: --after my-- September 3rd. I'm sorry. September. I have-- May 3rd is Constitution Day, so it's in my head. [LAUGHTER] September 1st-- 3rd in the morning, early in the morning. And I walk from the railroad station, which was a very close distance, about 3/4 mile-- kilometers, not mile-- to my home. And, of course, they were all up already then. And my mother almost fainted when she saw me, because she just gived up. She knew it was 3 and 1/2 kilometers. The Germans actually made it the same day to Chorzow. The city itself was not destroyed in 1939. It was no destruction. But as soon as they came, I found out later that the synagogue in Chorzow-- was a beautiful synagogue in Chorzow-- and that was burned a day after the Germans came. They did not wait for anything more. That synagogue was down in 1939 in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, when you arrived home, the Germans were there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in my home town. The Germans were not in my home town. And-- but we knew that the front line is very close. So I knew. My uncles knew. My mother knew. My grandparents knew. Two of the uncles decided to seek relief and go into a safer spot. And we were supposed to have just driven by horse and wagon. We supposed to have just driven 21 kilometers near the River Vistula. That's how far it was-- 21 kilometers. And we thought the German army will never to be able to cross that river. It's a big river. And never be crossed, the river. And that we'd be safe, and eventually come back. The war will be over in few weeks, a month, a month or something like that. Then we come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on that-- on that horse and wagon, there were three of my relatives. Three uncles-- all three brothers of my mother. There was the professor-- the professor of religion, Dr. Landfish

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a family. My uncle David married into a family by the name of [? Newmark. ?] And they had the horses. They had two giant horses and a big, big wagon. And the whole-- all those people were able to get on that wagon. They was-- let me count how many there were. Myself, three, five, eight, eight. Dr. Landfish and his wife is 10. OK. And the [? Newmark ?] family consisting of five people. Everybody was able to get out on that-- on that wagon. And we started out under-- I started out-- I thought I would get home, and that will be the end of it. I would relax, wash myself, and stay home with my parents. Because that meant my mother and my three brothers. We did not know into what we going into. We thought we going to go 21 miles, and that will be it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how was it decided that you would go, but your mother and brothers wouldn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother didn't want to go, I was the oldest in my house. And that nothing will happen to us, go with your uncles. The reason with my uncle was-- were-- my-- my uncle and my aunt, actually, decided to go. And just because she decided to go, and she urge everybody to go, because she was thrown out from Germany in 1938. If she wouldn't have been there-- and by the way, they just came also from vacation. Everybody was on vacation that time. They had-- they married in February, and they couldn't have a honeymoon because of the time, the season, whatever it is. So they decided to go in the summer. So they went on vacation. And they just made it, actually. They just made it a day before I did from [POLISH]. They just made it. And because of her, really, because of her-- and her name was Mona-- is Mona Lehrfeld. Because of her, because she suffered already from German sense, she didn't trust the Germans. The other people, like other my uncle, my mother-- first of all, she had her parents there. She wouldn't leave her parents there. She had a sister there. She wouldn't leave her sister there with the three children. And my other uncle, Moniek, she-- they have a baby, and they were fluent in German. He says, we don't have to be afraid. You know, we can talk to German people and, you know, the soldiers. It wasn't so bad. And the same thing with my grandfather. He said, it wasn't so bad in the first World War. We had-- we didn't suffer from the German hands. And that was-- that was the main reason, really, which so many people stayed behind. Because they trusted the German that would not it happen, because the Germans were good in 1920 or '18, or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- because-- only because she urged everybody to go, I don't trust the Germans, we went. And my mother said, go with your two uncles. You'll be-- the war will be over. You will come back. We never realized what will be the end of the whole thing. So in any event, we started out. And we came through that town, and by the time we reached that town, the Germans were practically on the other side. Practically on the other side. And as a matter of fact, we heard the artillery cannons and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German planes scrupulously were just attacking, no matter what-- children. There were no armies there. There were just civilians. They were just shooting down with machine guns. They were not even bombarding. They were just running through, because there was no defense against those planes. The army was practically gone. And that was-- you know, it was a rural area, so they didn't have any defense. So they simply lowered down the planes, and just with the machine guns were shooting down at you. It was terrible. In any event, we continued to go. Whenever we came, the Germans were close by. A couple days later, we came to a little shtetl, a little small town by the name of Belz. And we stay. We came there during the night, and we slept on the-- on the ground overnight. And the, the following morning, we knock at the door, and they were very Hasidic town. Belz was a very Hasidic town. They opened up the door. We asked strictly for water to get washed, and we got the water. And again, we started out again. And we continued. We continued for 12 days until we got to a large city by the name of Lwow. And we stopped there. That was a large city. The front line was still away from Lwow. The Germans were not close there, and we stopped there. Now, Lwow was not far away. As far as I remember, it could have been about 100 kilometer away from the-- from the Russian border. It was the farthest town. Let me continue on that. So we stopped in Lwow. Then, a couple days later, the Germans and the Russians entered a agreement, a pact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russia declared-- the Soviet Union attacked and declared war on Poland. And, of course, it didn't take them long. They occupied city of Lwow. And they annexed that territory there, and we get-- I got stuck together with my-- rest of the-- of my family in Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were living in Lwow for one week when the Russian army all of a sudden came in and annexed that-- the-- they proceeded to continue, you know, more east. And we were stuck in Lwow. Didn't know anything what will develop at that particular time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were living in Lwow till May of 1940. Now how did you live then? How did you support yourself under the Russians there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the Russians there, we'd strictly support ourselves. Whatever we had, we sold and-- to buy bread. We were able to find some little things to do. Not much, not much. Little things. One of my uncles were able to find a job. Two of my uncles, actually, were able to find a job. And we just were pitching in. We still had, you know, money here and there. And whatever we had, we just simply spent--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to find housing?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on bread, on bread. We were able to house-- find housing through the Jewish committee-- through the Jewish committee-- community, which was living there. There was a substantial amount of Jewish communities living in Lwow. And there was substantial amount of apartments there. So we were able to find apartments. And that was apartment-- the whole family moved into one apartment. Apartment, in, in some instances, consisted only from one large room and a kitchen. And that was fine, as long as we had the floor to sleep on. That was perfectly OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were able to find-- we were-- let me, see four families, actually. Let me count them. There were four families, close families living in that particular apartment. There was only one large room and a kitchen. That's all. No furniture there-- nothing. We didn't intend to live there, so nobody was buying any furniture. Secondly, we wouldn't have any money for the furniture. We were glad that we had money for bread. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and we were getting also help from the Jewish community. We were getting help, as much as they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many refugees in Lwow like you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that the Jewish community was helping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. There were many community there. There were a lot of-- lot of Jewish refugees there. In thousands, I don't know. But in my estimation-- in my estimation, were at least 50,000 people. At least 50,000-- probably more. And people were helping. People were helping. Of course, in the beginning, nobody knew how long it will take. Later on, got a little bit harder for the people. Because when Russia came in, everything stopped. Everything stopped. In other words, you know, they right away took the businesses away from the people. They didn't wait long. They took the businesses away from the people, put the business owners in jail. They didn't-- they, they were not ashamed of that. So life got disrupted there. And, of course, you know, the help was a little bit lesser. But thank God, we didn't stay there long. We stayed there only until May, but we made it. Meanwhile, in spring-- in spring of 1940, there was a registration for the people from the east to go back home. So, of course, 99% people registered to go home. And actually, that was a-- not a true registration. It was registration by the Russians, but the KBG.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't [INAUDIBLE] those days. We didn't know what, so we trusted everybody. You want to go home? Of course, I want to go home. You didn't know what was going on at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- we didn't know. No. 1940 was still OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard from your mother at all? Were you able to communicate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from Lwow, I was able to communicate with my mother with just writing cards. Just postal card. Because at that time, was still OK. That time, was still OK. It was-- in my city, it's still-- in 1941, as late as 1941, I was already in Siberia. And I was still able to receive from my mother two postal cards. In one of them, she asked me if I want to have a package, but we will come to that later. So in 1940, nobody knew anything. Was fine. Was fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you registered to go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have any yellow stars wearing, nothing. So, of course, I registered. Both of my uncles registered, and the other ones also registered to go home. The trick was that the Russian were looking for addresses where people are living. They cannot just go from one house to the other and at random knock on the door if they don't know who is from the east. They didn't want to approach the people which are residents of Lwow. They we're only interested of people which came from the east. In other words, from Cracow or whatever the-- from Warsaw, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we fell for it. And, of course, we registered. We didn't know it was a trick. And the-- I think the registration was over. And it lasted till May of 1940. And in May-- middle of May of 1940, there was a knock on the door middle of the night-- always middle of the night. Everybody was asleep. And the KBG knock on the door and called up names, of course. Put your clothes on and come on down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just simple as that. No explanation. Just put your clothes down, and come on down. So they took us to a-- downstairs into a trucks. And from the trucks, they had a secluded area where the transport plane was standing. I mean, transport train was standing. And they put us in the transport trains and the wagons. There were about between 15-- 50 and 60 people in it. And that was the end of Lwow for me. And we were underway, of course, not knowing where. And eventually after three weeks, we found ourself in the European Siberia, in the central Siberia, which was approximately 300 kilometers from-- I only know by kilometers. [LAUGHS] 300 kilometers from the Ural Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the train like that you were sent on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train was just-- what do you call it? Plain wagons. Not a passenger wagon, just a transport wagons. Usually, they carry cattles in this thing here with little windows. So you would be-- get a little air in there. And that's it. There was nothing in there. There was no toilet facilities. Absolutely nothing. And after we got in, the door was locked. And finally, about a day and a half later, the door was open in a remote area. And then the KBG open up the thing, with a pointed bayonet at-- like criminals. And they supplied us with water, and some soup, and some bread. And that was the first meal we had in about an hour-- in about a day and a half. Of course, we took some bread with us-- whatever we had from that apartment. So we didn't really starve, but it was just very hard. And the water, which we didn't have. The only thing which we were missing really is water. And the toilet facilities. There was one baby there-- a little tiny baby. The baby was not even half a year old. And as a matter of fact, they survived. The whole family survived with the baby. I was at the baby's wedding at one point. [LAUGHS] So-- and we got friendly with that family. And we stayed friendly throughout the years. Of course, one of the-- as a matter of fact, the baby's father is a co-signer on my-- on-- as a witness to my wedding. He was also in the army, and I supported his regiment. So that's how close we got. We survived both the war and survived the labor camp in Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any event, after three weeks, we arrived to our destination, which was [RUSSIAN] Republic. And nothing to see-- just tremendous trees. There was nothing there-- no industry, no nothing. The only industry was forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dilapidated homes, single family, dilapidated homes. A single family consisted of one communal kitchen. No water, no electricity no heating except for that wooden stove, and temperature dropping to 65 below zero. [LAUGHS] Anyway, we made it. So my job, of course-- I was very lucky, because I was still underage. One of my cousins was a single man. He was living with us. He was a single man, and he was three years older than I am. And he was sent away to a completely different camp. And he suffered such a terrible pains, it's unbelievable. He made it, but it reflects on his life now. He made it, but it reflects on his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any event, we were settled in. Got one-- one little home, OK. One room, no matter how many people you were. We had-- in our room, we had four-- six people. Four-- six people in our one room, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, it consisted from four different families. In other words, two of my-- two-- one of my brother-in-law with his wife. Then one of my-- my-- by the way, my sister-in-law at that particular time was pregnant, and that was the first baby was born in that camp. He survived. Was very sick throughout the years, but he survived. He survived, and he become the vice president of Philip Morris. So he survived very well. In any event, so it was my-- my brother-in-law and his wife, my uncle and his wife. They were-- they didn't have any children. Myself, and, of course, my future wife. So I consider that four different families, because we were stranger at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were 16 people living altogether in that-- 16 or 18 people in that particular little house with one kitchen. There were how many couples? Let me see-- one, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two couple-- that's three. And that one couple had five children. And then another couple-- yeah, another couple with two-- with three children. So many couples were living in that particular house-- each one in a room. That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, that one couple which was only a man and a wife, there was no room for them, so they had to sleep in the kitchen. On the floor, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everyone taken from the same place, from Lwow? Or were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They all were taken from the same place in Lwow-- not from the same building. We all met, except for myself, my uncle, and my brother-in-law-- as-- as later developed, he became my brother-in-law, because I married my-- my sister-in-law's sister, so we became brother-in-law. But at that particular time, we were strangers. They all came from different area in Lwow. We didn't know each other, except for that-- the six-- I mean, those four people which we knew. The rest were all strangers. And that was it there. And our job-- a day later, we got assignments. They put us together on a-- you know, with-- that they didn't call everybody in, just, you know, house by house. And they tell us what our assignments will be. And the first warning was, forget about-- they were asking us from what town we are. And just forget about this town. From now on, this town, you're going to build right here in this area. Forget about a home. That's your home. And they assigned us to take the trees down. The forest-- not trees. Giant trees, giant forest. No-- there was not a-- people who didn't-- had a foot in this thing, in that forest, when they brought us to that section there. It was so dense that you couldn't see nothing. And in that forest, actually, we had bears and wolves. They were living in that area there. But in any event, we were assigned to take those trees down. And each one has different assignment. And so, that's what we were doing-- taking down those trees manually, because no electric saws, no any technique. They just put on a foreman, which was also a KBG man. And he showed us how to do it, and he teached us. You look at the top of the tree, he said, and see in what direction that tree leans. And that tree has to go that direction. You don't cut the tree in the opposite direction, because it goes on your head. So that's-- that was the lessons which we got. Nobody knew how to chop wood. [LAUGHS] Nobody knew how to chop trees. But nevertheless, we got used to it. And we were chopping those trees down until 19-- until the end of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the trees go after you chopped them down?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trees were simply-- he-- they told us-- by the way, in 1941, they war broke out. But at that time, we didn't know any-- any-- any war, so the trees, some of them, the smaller trees, were going for coal mines. And they were cut, I think, you know, the height of the-- of the coal mine. In other words, the height-- the height, I think, was eight feet, or something like that. In feet. Was about eight feet from the ground. And it called [RUSSIAN]. You had to clean that tree-- that tree, strip it from, from the core, you know, which is the outside of the tree. Completely clean. And most of it went for telephone poles. And most of them also went were pine trees. Pine trees-- not any hardwood, just pine-- went for, for boards or something, for building material.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the-- when the war started in 1941, they were-- we were told that you better work a little bit harder, because those trees will have-- we have to use for constructing bridges, OK? Ships, and all kind of things. So we're using it for the-- for the material-- for the war material. So that-- that's where it was going. But originally, it started with telephone poles, and cleaning for the mills, and coal mines-- that was the main thing. Now, where did your food come from here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was, was that-- we did not have any kitchen as such. We didn't get any raw food. There was a cafeteria, and everybody was fed in the cafeteria. As far as the food, there was no meat. We didn't see any meat. There-- you always were given bread, but it was no fresh bread. It was dry bread. Dry-- they call it [RUSSIAN]. In other words, it's not like toast. It was simply dried with heat. And it was, you know, from the kitchen. From the kitchen. And soup-- mostly soups and bread, and something like that. There was very little milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: We better stop this tape now and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
